item.title: Topolski's Chronicle, V.3 No.3-4, 1955
item.creator: Topolski, Feliks, 1907-1989
item.description: Topolski's Chronicle is a series of broadsheets created by Feliks Topolski and distributed bimonthly to subscribers from 1953 through 1982. Topolski was a superlative draughtsman and covered current events with an artistic, unique style.

pages.typeset_text:
UNIVERSITY OF UTAH LIBRARIES  TOPOLSKI'S CHRONICLE No. 3-4 (39-40) Vol. III 1555
THE ARTIST IN MODERN SOCIETY
THE HEADING AND THEME OF THIS ISSUE ARE TAKEN FROM THE UNESCO REPORT ON ITS RECENT INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE OF ARTISTS. AS THIS CHRONICLE BELIEVES THAT SUCH A CONFERENCE NEEDS TO BE CONTINUED BY THE RANK-AND-FILE IN THE STUDIOS, GALLERIES, SCHOOLS, INSTITUTES, CAFES AND ALSO IN PRINT, AWAY FROM THE BIAS OF NATIONAL PIGEON-HOLING, AND THE CHRONICLE'S GENERAL SURVEY OF THE ARTIST'S PLACE IN SOCIETY IS DUE.
sketch_descriptions: Expressive group portrait of three figures, featuring a large, imposing man with a monocle or pendant, a woman with an elaborate hairstyle, and a lean man with exaggerated features and spectacles standing close together.
A looser figure seated or crouched in the right foreground, partially glanced over by the main group.
pages.page_summary: This page introduces the theme of the artist's role in modern society, referencing the UNESCO International Conference of Artists alongside an expressive group portrait by Felix Topolski. The editorial emphasizes ongoing discussion about artists' societal place beyond national or institutional boundaries.

pages.typeset_text: A Paper read at the invitation of THE ARTS AND AMENITIES GROUP OF THE PARLIAMENTARY LABOUR PARTY at THE HOUSE OF COMMONS on the subject of THE CITIZEN-ARTIST ... In 1948, at the time of 'Labour the Ever,' I wrote in THE NEW STATESMAN ...
pages.handwritten_text: Portrait of Paul Gauguin by Schuff 1889. Fire-chalk—22½ x 17 inches (0. 34 x 0. 29 m.) Signed on the edge of the frame. Lent by Mme. S[...]ar[...]e Caclin-Schlanc, Paris.  The overheard conversation—remarks quoted above do not necessarily reflect the editor’s opinion and, noting in the usual overcrowding, at a Private View, their authenticity is uncertain. Not to Talk. [...]
sketch_descriptions: Expressive black ink sketch of a crowd of people standing closely together, possibly attending an art viewing or gallery event.
Detailed profile portrait in red circle, likely representing Paul Gauguin.
Small group of standing figures, some engaged in conversation on the upper right of the page, rendered in quick fluid lines.
Scene of a person addressing an audience or class, with additional figures seated behind books or desks, and shelves or a display labeled 'MODERN ART'.

events: A Paper read at the invitation of THE ARTS AND AMENITIES GROUP OF THE PARLIAMENTARY LABOUR PARTY

pages.page_summary: This page from Topolski's Chronicle blends editorial text and sketches reflecting on the role of the 'citizen-artist,' framed by scenes and commentary from art events and public discourse, and featuring a portrait of Paul Gauguin. The content is set against a backdrop of mid-20th-century British parliamentary and artistic engagement.

pages.typeset_text:
PATRONS OF ART, LITERATURE, THEATRE AND MUSIC
341. Sponsored exhibition of 'Modern Art'.
342. Dealers in Old Masters.
343. 'Oh, but he tells I'm not an abstractionist...'
344. Panel-show producer and gentlemen at a country festival.
345. Poetry reading in an East End pub.
346. Aristocratic patron as lion plays one of the 'little' London theatres.
347. 'Playwright addresses the Critics' Circle. "Leo is the head and the brains of big criticism."'
348. Music-patroness and the 'permanent lamentable player'.
349. A literary magazine-lunching party.
350. Booksellers' 'Literary Luncheon'.
351. Service and the wife and daughter of his newspaper proprietor.
pages.handwritten_text:
What are we writing? The underground?
Lady Rampshire Lucy Mr[?]
Hutchinson HAW. V. CONNIE CRACK
English Literary Luncheon. Mr. W. L. Ambles[?]
Sir Allen - Peter[?]
sketch_descriptions: A crowd of people observing or participating in a 'Modern Art' exhibition.
Two women dressed in period costume speaking with a man by a painting, referencing Old Masters art dealers.
A small group engaged in conversation, with one woman exclaiming to another in animated fashion.
Three men gathered at a table, with one central figure—likely the panel-show producer—gesturing with papers at a country festival.
A busy pub interior with a man reading poetry to an attentive audience.
An austere aristocratic figure and seated audience at a small London theatre, with a majestic lion looming.
A central figure addressing a seated group, with heavy vertical lines suggesting a formal venue, referencing a playwright and the Critics' Circle.
Two figures, a woman and a man, are close and engaged, referencing a 'music-patroness' and a melancholy musician.
A lively party scene with multiple figures, referencing a literary magazine lunch.
A formal gathering with seated figures, one possibly reading from a paper, referencing a booksellers' literary luncheon.
A trio comprising a man identified as 'Service', and two women, one noted as a newspaper proprietor's wife or daughter.
pages.page_summary: This page documents the social scenes, personalities, and gatherings surrounding British art, literature, theatre, and music in the mid-20th century. Through sketches and captions, it highlights the varied patrons, events, and cultural milieus sustaining the arts.

pages.typeset_text:
The Festival of Britain was a magnificent show of the country's resilience and creativity, showcasing numerous contributions to the arts, education, and national identity. The artist is hailed as a prime contributor, but their exact function—whether as commentator or producer, innovator or craftsman—remains a subject of extensive debate. The Times editorial discusses the role of art and artists in society, highlighting artists' marginal position and the effects of state support, academies, and state-determined commissions. The article further explores education in arts, the bureaucracy surrounding arts funding, and how these relate to the authenticity and vitality of artistic output. The Times then explores three types of persons with relation to art: The Audience (Promenaders of Raphielites), The Official (The Minister of Arts), and The Enthusiasts (The Art Associators). 

INTERNATIONAL RAPPROCHEMENTS
The contemporary Mammoth Powers find safety on the national projecting through parties, alliances, collective action, and a tightening of the United Nations family fabric. In the scope of this struggle, special mention is made of UNESCO for its efforts at world cultural rapprochement. Noted in the journal is the visit of the Paris-based UNESCO Director-General Luther Evans, as guest of the British National Commission, in association with the government of the Federal Republic. The interview he found, in what British policy regards as proper, has just had another share put to the title: the 'policy of national parties.'

411 Director-General of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation at a reception. 412 Young artists at "Les Courses de France." 413 Soviet composer Khatchaturian. 414 Reception at London hotels. 415 Second drawing of Director-General Luther Evans.
pages.handwritten_text:
Luther Evans, Director General - UNESCO
I AM KHACHATURIAN - Soviet composer at the London Int. Conference Arts
MY 7th symphony is a must for... Hungarians. My new ballet, 'Gayane,' is performed...
sketch_descriptions: Upper right large sketch of a toread man in a suit gesturing, identified as Luther Evans, at an official or diplomatic event.
Smaller sketch in profile of a seated woman in elaborate clothes and hat, possibly at the same diplomatic or cultural reception.
Group portrait lower left of three men, one younger, two older, possibly representing 'The Audience', 'The Official', and 'The Enthusiast' as discussed in the text.
Panoramic sketch of a room with young artists seated, likely engaged in discussion or critique, titled 'Young artists in Les Courses de France.'
Central-right bust portrait of a man labeled Khachaturian, animatedly speaking to a listener, the scene marked as being at the London International Conference on the Arts.
Multiple small scenes lower right: a man and woman in conversation, additional small figures in social interaction, likely at a reception or conference.

events: London International Conference on the Arts

pages.page_summary: This page from Topolski's Chronicle documents cultural diplomacy and the international arts scene of the early 1950s, focusing on UNESCO's efforts and the Festival of Britain, with expressive sketches of prominent figures such as Luther Evans and Khachaturian.

pages.typeset_text: LATEST NEWS FROM THE CAVES AND VOIDS ITEM: The Apologists’ retreat down to the CAVES, in search of the perpetuity of Pure Form, has failed: ART at its source is not FORM but the act of forming. The constant and visible “sign” of the “informed flesh” of Man’s “blood” (the terminologies of Jung, the psycho-analyst, and the Gottschalk thinker, join in the prompter of our Civilization). ITEM: The disenchanted, split soul in the old and bloodless world, and the “freedom” forced on the world at the present moment of mankind—by the vast mechanics of the “movement” paradoxically neglected at home—spread all over the globe as the signal event of Western Materialism, are both a permanent and unrecognized international vice of local “provincialism.” The rules come at the heels of Freedom. ITEM: Art of “avaggish” does well in moderate climates. ITEM: “Social” and “Royal” Brazilians very well. The price of this double issue is 1s. ‘TOPOLSKI’S CHRONICLE’ IS PUBLISHED 24 TIMES A YEAR AT 2s. 6d. A COPY YEARLY SUBSCRIPTION RATES (WITH FOLDER AND INDEX) TO ANY PART OF THE WORLD ARE L3. 3s (39s or equivalent). SHORTER PERIODS PRO RATA. AIR MAIL BY QUOTATION. SUBSCRIPTIONS FROM OUTSIDE THE STERLING AREA MAY BE PAID THROUGH THE UNESCO COUPON SCHEME. Handprinted in Great Britain by G. Beelanczyk, 146 Sutton Walk, S.E.1, from blocks made by Austin Moles Ltd and published from Bridge Arch 185, Sutton Walk, South Bank, London, S.F.1 Copyright reserved.
pages.handwritten_text: Kandinsky his very [[democratic?]] way of storytelling in the new aesthetic (which I found when... [unclear] ...intellectually honest when he grouped, as far as possible, both movements for the same dissatisfaction). My chairs are close together. Bernard Dorival, curator of the Museum of Modern Art, Museum of the People. Public Libraries of France, just arrived for normal use of painting.... to Mr. F.H. Seager, director of Manchester Public Libraries for the records and galleries in Paris who responded magnificently to the two requests... Professor EDEL DAMOGLOU. November [..] myself to myself for the calls after... That's what, being him really means... Compliment to Prof. in your [notebook?] handwritten within the room.
sketch_descriptions: A crowd of figures gathered and gesturing energetically, possibly in a celebratory or performative scene, with gestural marks creating a sense of liveliness (India).
A group of four figures, some with hats and one with a camera or binoculars, near a sign reading 'GROTTE LASCAUX' (France).
Several people, including a police officer, closely observing framed artworks on a gallery wall (Tokyo).
Two levels of figures moving between staircases and art on display, capturing dynamic movement through a busy gallery space (New York).
Two people, one in uniform or official attire, discussing or pointing at art in a gallery setting, accompanied by handwritten commentary (London).
pages.page_summary: This page from Topolski's Chronicle presents international scenes of art viewing and appreciation in places such as India, France, Tokyo, New York, and London, alongside reflections on art's meaning and recent trends. The sketches and notes juxtapose local art world gatherings with philosophical musings and tributes to notable figures in the cultural field.
